223505024.review: I regret having to post a negative review of a game, but this really took me by surprise. I saw on multiple places (reddit, forums, etc.) that Visage was some people's favorite horror game due to how scary the game was, and unfortunately that was not my experience. At best, this is a 6.5/10 game to me. There were admittedly a handful of genuinely scary parts, but otherwise the game was somewhat frustrating to play. There are four chapters, and each chapter the design is pretty similar: you're learning about a tragic thing that happened to someone who lived in this house before you, and you have to solve puzzles while always having a looming threat of a monster chasing you. Except the monster can appear randomly--really anywhere--which means that it could spawn right next to you, and you only have a second or two to react. Meaning sometimes the "gameplay loop" consists of you repeatedly dying (in a fairly loud and lengthy cutscene for the purpose it serves, I might add) in an effort to determine what miniscule detail you missed. I read guides online as to how to progress and perhaps the game was updated, but closing the door on a monster never had an effect because all of them seem to be able to warp through doors, meaning you have to line-of-sight each monster, rendering the doorways useless. Lastly, while I appreciate a modern game that eschews the "go here, do this" markers or compass, I think Visage is overly obtuse with its direction. For example, I spent the first two hours of gameplay exploring every inch of the house and finding secrets, which was fun but ultimately led to a game of "spot the difference" later on which was tedious and ultimately not fun, and vastly different from how the actual gameplay was in the chapters proper. I think there's other games that, both in terms of narrative and gameplay, do a better job, and therefore I cannot recommend this game at its current price.

222578606.review:
Visage came highly recommended to me, with a lot of people claiming it’s one of—if not the—scariest games out there.

And I will say… it’s a great game.

Was it the scariest game I’ve ever played? No, not for me personally. But I still genuinely enjoyed it.

The atmosphere is where Visage really stands out. It’s incredibly creepy, with this constant tension that makes you hesitate before walking around every corner. You never feel fully comfortable, and the game does a great job of keeping you in that uneasy, on-edge state the entire time.

The sound design plays a huge role in that. The way things move, shift, and happen around you keeps you guessing what’s coming next. It’s not always about what you see—it’s what you think might happen that really gets to you.

I also really liked how the story is structured.

You play as someone living in a house, uncovering the stories of the people who lived there before you. There are three different storylines you follow and investigate, and I thought that concept was really cool. Each one brings something different to the table, and overall, the stories themselves were interesting and well done.

So with all of that… why didn’t this completely blow me away?

For me, it mostly comes down to the pacing.

There’s a lot of walking back and forth, a lot of searching for items, and at times it can feel a bit tedious. The game leans heavily into tension and uneasiness—which it does very well—but there aren’t a ton of jumpscares to break that up. And while that works for a lot of people, I found myself wishing for just a little bit more to keep things from feeling stretched in certain moments.

Don’t get me wrong—the game is still fantastic.

But I think the amount of hype around it set my expectations really high, and because of that, I walked away just slightly wanting more.

For me, this sits more around a 9.5 rather than a perfect 10.

I absolutely recommend Visage, especially if you love slow, psychological horror and heavy atmosphere. Just go in knowing it’s more about tension than constant scares—and you’ll probably have a great time with it. 👀

221923135.review:
First, let's get the cons about this game out of the way. 

The fact that this was a Kickstarter project is apparent specific sections. I'll refrain from saying which as to not spoil anything but it felt apparent to me, namely one section where the game would consistently crash unless I used a cheeky save-scummy workaround. The developers were NOT kidding when they say this game is difficult. You need to pay attention to what is revealed to you, as some clues will only be revealed once and your progress hinges on the fact you can retain a lot of info without immediately having context as to what it will be useful for. Lateral thinking will help you immensely (duh, puzzles in a Silent Hill-inspired horror game), but even then I found some solutions a touch contrived -- especially when fighting for my life for 15-30 minutes to just collect the items I need to try the solution I've cooked up, certain that the logic is sound just to find I set myself on a fool's errand. Heck, there is one puzzle I would even say is nearly trial and error if it weren't for certain tells -- again, won't say where. 

Now, the positives.

That all said, I was immensely pleased with the whole of this game despite all this, and ultimately it stuck its landing. It wears its influence on its sleeve proudly, getting so close to being the "Silent Hills" we never got. While I can't say it is one-to-one with any specific Silent Hill, the closest being PT and 4, it is a spectacular project with so much polish put into it that you can easily forgive the few areas where it is lacking. If anything, that polish makes those few sections stick out like a sore thumb, and I would so dearly love to see this game patched at some point to fix those few issues. It would easily be a near perfect game for the old-school psychological horror fans, should it ever get that last touch-up I feel it deserves.

It won't be for everyone. The game is unforgiving at times, sometimes almost unfairly. But almost never unintentionally -- after all, you deserve to be here. Allow it to steep you in its world, whether on your own terms or the game's, and you will be surprised by how far it will take you.

Highly recommended. But don't be afraid to check a walkthrough, if you absolutely need to.

214521338.review:
I'm super disappointed that I have to make this a negative review, but this game has too many flaws to overlook.

[h2] Pros [/h2]
[b]Very Good Atmosphere[/b] - As this is essentially "P.T.: Expanded" it nails the atmosphere behind the original. You can tell that this house is wrong (if the first cutscene didn't already clue you in at that point) and the different sections of each chapter solidify that.

[b]Dynamic Sanity System[/b] - The system works by triggering more paranormal events (lights turning off/breaking, doors closing, etc.) the lower sanity you are. This can snowball because those events lower your sanity, which causes more events + can summon a ghost that kills you.
[hr][/hr]
[h2] Cons [/h2]
[b]Mind-numbingly Slow[/b] - When exploring the main house, you move slower than the guy who's literally in crutches. It takes about a minute and a half to move from the second floor parent's bedroom to the power room in the basement, and during the chapter with the old woman, you have to go through a 30 second long cutscene to get to the second floor. [b] You have to go through this cutscene constantly because you have to backtrack. [/b]

[b]Inconsistent Danger[/b] Ghosts seemingly spawn at random rather than being tied to how much sanity you have. This means that (more frequently in my playthrough, I should mention!) you can [i] randomly die because the ghost appeared directly in front of you.[/i]
Even then, at no point in the game did I feel I was ever "in danger" from a ghost that [i]was[/i] chasing me, because (even with your slow speed) you move way faster than them. So much faster, I was able to get out of a dead end by juking the ghost.
Also, not once was I ever worried about my sanity level, because it is both laughably easy to get back sanity (just look at a window or a light) or take one of the many pills you'll find in the house.
I'm not even joking when I say there's a room "that you get the key to super early on" that has [b] Nine [/b] lightbulbs and [b] Four [/b] lighters. I should also mention not once did a single light fixture break except for the scripted one at the very beginning; and that I only ran out of fuel for one lighter.

[b]"Needle-in-the-Haystack Objectives"[/b]
It is nigh-impossible to figure out what you need to do in both the mirror chapter and during the last leg of the game. For example, [spoiler] the Affliction tape needs you to: Grab the crowbar, Go into the room with the covered up furniture, then break the floor. The Greed tape needs you to: Go to the boiler room, climb (and fail to) open a vent, get a stepladder from another room, then go through the vent. [/spoiler]
You are supposed to figure these out from just a picture of a well, and a picture of a contract respectively.
The game does not tell you [i] anything[/i] about what you need to do, which is especially bad since in some of these sections you are actively getting hunted by ghosts.

[b]Awkward Controls + Janky Inventory[/b]
Now, a janky inventory can be a good thing; see Receiver 2. The idea of having two extra slots because of your hands is interesting, but is implemented in such an awful way. 
You have to [i] hold [/i] down the drop key, [i] then [/i] click which hand you want to drop from with your mouse buttons.
Instead of just opening doors with a click, you have to hold down your mouse and drag the door open Amnesia-style. Thing is, it works in Amnesia because stealth is an integral mechanic. Here, it's pointless and frustrating.
In order to open a locked door, instead of just going up to the door and clicking on it while having the key in possession, you instead have to:
Look at the door, open inventory, select the specific key (which unlocks the door), close inventory, [i] then [/i]you can go through the door.
[b]Good luck doing that on a controller while you're being chased. [/b]

[b]Lackluster Ending[/b]
[h1] This section includes spoilers for the "Family Reunion" Ending. [/h1]

So you've gone through the 3 layers of (metaphorical) hell. You've gone through the 7 VHS tapes and completed the mask. Now it's time to leave this house.

You pick up the mask, then go back downstairs to the hidden room behind the painted wall in the basement. You open the door into a white void.

The silhouette of your family in front of you appears for a moment, then you're pulled backwards into...

Roll Credits.

...what?

[b]You're telling me I spent 8 hours of my life slogging through this game for it to[/b] 
[url=https://www.reddit.com/r/familyguy/comments/1nuk9vw/family_guys_obsession_with_the_ending_of_the/] end on a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ cut-to-black? [/url]
[hr][/hr]

Overall, it has an interesting premise, atmosphere, and story. But the gameplay is such a turnoff that I wouldn't find it hard to imagine someone else refunding just from that alone.

The Game Is Nearly Unplayable Due to UX and Controls. 
Despite its atmosphere, Visage is almost unplayable because of its UX and control design.
The only truly strong aspect of the game is the environmental art and mood. Everything else, especially the interaction system, feels extremely unpolished, inconsistent, and confusing. It genuinely comes across as if the core mechanics were put together by someone taking their first lessons with Blueprints.

Main issues:

Interactions are unclear, inconsistent, and frustrating. You never know what the game wants from you, and basic actions feel like guesswork.

The controls keep trying to “reinvent the wheel.” Instead of following proven standards in first-person horror, the game introduces odd, experimental interaction methods that only break immersion and flow.

Feedback is almost non-existent. Whether an item can be used, examined, or picked up often feels random.

The obscurity doesn’t enhance horror — it just creates friction.
It’s not scary; it’s confusing.

It’s honestly a shame, because the ambience, lighting, sound, and level design are excellent. But the foundation of the gameplay — the part that should make the experience truly engaging — is so poorly implemented that it overshadows everything the game does well.

If the UX and controls were rebuilt following basic interaction patterns used across the genre, Visage could be a nice horror game. Right now, it feels held back by design decisions that make it far more frustrating than frightening.

I really wanted to like Visage. It has a reputation for being the spiritual successor to P.T., and visually, it looks great. However, I ended up dropping the game before the end because it was simply too frustrating to enjoy.

[h1]Pros[/h1]
[list]
[*] [b]The Story Concept:[/b] The narrative is genuinely dark, twisted, and interesting—I just wish it was told better.
[*] [b]Graphics & Visuals:[/b] The game looks incredible. The environment is photorealistic, and the lighting effects are top-notch.
[*] [b]Sound Design:[/b] The audio creates a very tense atmosphere. The creaks, whispers, and ambient noises are very effective.
[*] [b]Scare Factor:[/b] In the beginning (before the frustration set in), the game does have some genuinely scary moments.
[/list]

[h1]Cons:[/h1]
[list]
[*] [b]Boring Gameplay:[/b] The pacing is incredibly slow. Too much time is spent wandering aimlessly trying to figure out what to do next.
[*] [b]Terrible Controls:[/b] Opening doors, picking up items, and interacting with the world feels clunky and unresponsive.
[*] [b]Inventory Management:[/b] This is the worst offender. The inventory is convoluted, hard to navigate, and constantly fights against the player.
[/list]

Conclusion: Instead of feeling scared, I just felt bored and annoyed. I spent more time fighting the UI and the controls than I did enjoying the horror. I couldn't bring myself to finish it. Skip this unless you have infinite patience.

206769404.review:
A lukewarm recommendation. A game that really shows the flaws of a binary rating system. If it was an option, i'd give the game a thumb sideways.

There are genuinely atmospheric and scary moments that are worth playing the game for. It earns its scares, and the "unearned" ones, such as jump scares, are justified because of the well done atmosphere of the house and the well crafted horror sequences. If it was a haunted house game, I'd give it a thumbs up with no hesitation. (Admittedly, 1/4 of the "scary" part of the game is not even that scary, unless you find hospital-horror to be scary.)

My hesitation comes from the fact that the game tries to have a story, Well, it has a story, but it's both not good and is also hidden behind a reliance on the player to interpret abstract gameplay sequences which are not scary, in a horror game.

It's like going to a real life haunted house, enjoying the scares, and being forced at the end of to casually walk through obviously non-dangerous rooms which explain the backstory, trauma, and meaning behind the rooms you just went through. I don't need to know that that one spooky chainsaw room in the haunted house is actually a representation of the effects of addiction on someone's loved ones, just let it scare me and leave me enjoying that I was scared.

It's honestly a fault of the medium. If this was a haunted house, you wouldn't need to include the walking simulator story sequences. You'd be in, out, and getting back in line to see the spooky rooms and costumed actors again. The fact that it's a game burdens the scares with also having to convey a story to justify the existence of the scares. 

It's like the game treats the enjoyable, scary moments as context for the *true* payoff, which is the abstract and "profound" story that comes after the scares. The game has it backwards. Instead, the story should exist to serve as a vehicle to deliver the player to the scares.

Sure, in this game, you'll get the fun of driving fast on the freeway, but know that the game is designed to force you to slow down and wait in traffic while you take one of its many off-ramps every few hours. And, sometimes, an entire gameplay sequence is just one long wait in traffic while you wait for the horror game to become scary again.

Definitely wait for this to be on sale.

One of the most frightening horror experiences through any medium that I have put myself through.  Considering the much smaller team and budget than Konami has, Sadsquare Studio did a wonderful job at filling the massive void left by the P.T./Silent Hills cancellation.   

The game can be a bit buggy and the inventory management is cumbersome like many have said, but you do get used to it, and the bugs are easy to overlook without breaking your immersion. The puzzles can be very tough and with little guidance. You really have to be patient and pay attention to any clue within your surroundings and throughout the house.

The scares are used effectively and rarely feel cheap. The atmosphere just drips with dread and foreboding throughout the whole game and does not reach a point where you feel "used to it," which is probably the highest compliment I can give to a horror game. An absolute MUST for any fan of the genre.  Playing in the dark with headphones is highly recommended.

205995278.review:
I've played this game for about 2 hours. It is enjoyable and i do plan to finish it. However, it has enough flaws that I wouldn't recommend it to people not very interested in haunted house games.

The main problem is that nothing makes any sense. There's a general idea of a family murder suicide and then a house that is sort of haunted afterward. But beyond that, eh. Yes, there are monsters in the house, and they are scary until you've seen them 100 times, but they don't realiy make any sense or relate to any particular story as far as I can tell.

You play as a person who sounds like a man, wandering around the house, collecting items, struggling to fit items in your pocket. The inventory system is a frustrating struggle. Compare similar horror games like Outlast or Layers of fear where you just click on an item to pick it up and that's that. 

And again the main issue is that there is no logic to what you are doing. The house is very big and there's a lot of rooms. Sometimes certain areas of the house will drain your fuel and sanity resources, sometimes they will not. Sometimes you have to go find a red door to advance the story, sometimes you have to click on a picture. It feels random and disconnected.

Are there good scares? Yes, there are a few, although the main "I'm going to kill you" monsters I think showed their face too much. Is the game well designed and polished? Unfortunately no.

So, I recommend this only to people who have played a lot of horror games and are willing to try one with a rough interface.

202338255.review:
I love horror, but I’ll admit, I have a hard time fully immersing myself sometimes (or maybe I’m just a bit of a scaredy cat). Visage feels heavily inspired by PT from way back when, and honestly, it does that legacy justice.

The game gives you multiple paths and stories to experience, ranging from absolutely horrific to “okay, that spooked me but I can breathe again.” No matter where you are, the ambiance is always there, keeping you on edge. The visuals do a ton of heavy lifting, the house feels too real, like it has a life of its own. Pair that with masterful sound design and you’ve got a recipe for constant paranoia.

This is one of those horror games that sticks with you long after you turn it off. It’s slow, deliberate, and absolutely drenched in atmosphere. The jump scares? Brutal, some of the best I’ve ever seen. It’s a masterclass in oppressive ambience, a real psychological gauntlet.

Gameplay is methodical but rewarding. You’ll explore, manage sanity, and piece together stories tied to the house. Some chapters lean more into psychological tension than others. The pacing can drag, and a few puzzles are so obscure they get frustrating, but the payoff is usually worth the effort.

Visage isn’t perfect, but it’s one of the most haunting, unforgettable horror experiences I’ve played. If you want a game that gets under your skin and refuses to leave, this is it.

200947101.review:
Because it's more expensive than a lot of indie horror games and had good reviews, I "saved" this one until I beat a few others. I was completely let down and this game sucks. They admit to being inspired by Amnesia: TDD, so I'll compare to that.
-Not being completely linear only works if you know what you're doing. Too many times already I was wandering forever to find an item I needed, completely ruining the flow of the game. In Amnesia everything was intelligently designed enough that you did wander, but eventually found what you were looking for. Here, I needed walkthroughs. Obviously the garage key is in the dryer, you idiot. Are you immersed yet???
-There's some okay tension, but a lot of jumpscares are cheap Amityville Horror ♥♥♥♥. After so much wandering, I'm so annoyed with the game that a door opening or a light bursting I could care less about. It also plays EXTREMELY slow and I feel like I'm moving through molasses everywhere. 
-The controls are horrible. Whoever created them needs flogged.
-The sanity system is cheap and not at all as in depth as Amnesia's felt. There's no slow descent into madness, just another annoying mechanic you suddenly die from. 
-There's no hiding from enemies, no stalking, no strategizing to get past them, not really even any good chases. Every once in a while you kinda just get randomly stabbed and die. What did you do wrong? Who knows. 
-The world is not interesting and the lore is barely there in some audio tapes and vague writings. While Amnesia's story and world slowly and brilliantly unfolded, here you're just trying to get.... uhhhhhh get some woman named Dolores' kid back. Why? Who knows. You can do chapters in any order, too, so there's very little continuity or respect for pacing. You're basically just wandering a funhouse and doing cheap sandbox horror. 
And, for some reason, the game kept launching on the wrong monitor and I kept having to switch it back. So ♥♥♥♥ you.
